coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical-assisted green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from crab shell as targeted nanoprobes for cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Dehvari, Khalilalrahman
Liu, Kai Yi
Tseng, Po-Jen
Gedda, Gangaraju
Girma, Wubshet Mekonnen
Chang, Jia-Yaw
coredata.dc:description: A one-pot sonochemical method was developed for the green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots with nitrogen dopants (N@C-dots) derived from crab shells. The synthesized N@C-dots exhibited excellent water solubility with a fluorescence quantum yield of 14.5%. The N@C-dots then conjugated with folic acid (FA) to construct the N@C-dots-FA nanoprobe with the ability to selectively target folate-receptor positive cancer cells. Moreover, in vitro cytotoxicity studies showed excellent biocompatibility. Cellular uptake of the N@C-dots-FA was further studied via in vitro confocal microscopy studies. Results showed that N@C-dots-FA nanoprobes were mostly localized in the cytoplasm of the folate receptor-overexpressing Human cervical cancer cell (HeLa) while cellular uptake by human embryo lung cell (HEL) cells (folate receptor-deficient control) was negligible. Based on their improved diagnostic imaging and enhanced targeting effect, the N@C-dots-FA probe has high potential in the theranostic applications. This study demonstrates an economically viable approach to synthesize N@C-dots-FA probe using seafood waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Crab shell
Green synthesis
Cell imaging
Sonochemical
Bio-waste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of N@C-dots with FA targeting ligand
N@C-dots-FA as a targeted probe for bio-imaging

This work was financially supported by the Taiwan Building Technology Center from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education in Taiwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N@C-dots fabrication from crab shells using sonochemical method.
(a) PL spectra of ground crab shells and synthesized N@C-dots applying different ultrasonic powers. (b) Raman measurements of ground crab shells and as-synthesized N@C-dots.
(a) HR-TEM image of N@C-dots. (b) and (c) images of individual N@C-dots (scale bar 2 nm). (d) The lattice-spacing profile of individual N@C-dots measured from the panel.
(a) XPS survey spectra of N@C-dots and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O1s, (c) N1s, and (d) C1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of samples (Insets show photographs of the N@C-dots in aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right). Emission spectra of an aqueous solution of (c) ground crab shells powder and (d) as-synthesized N@C-dots under different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of free FA, N@C-dots, and N@C-dots-FA. (b) TGA curves of the N@C-dots and N@C-dots -FA.

MTT assay for cell viability of HeLa cells after incubation with (a) N@C-dots and (b) N@C-dots-FA at different concentrations (0–1000 µg/mL) for 24 h. HeLa cells incubated with PBS were used as controls (0 µg/mL). Error bars indicate the mean ± standard deviations (n = 3).

In vitro confocal microscopic imaging of HeLa and HEL cells treated with N@C-dots-FA. (a) and (e) Bright-field images, (b) and (f) images of the cell nucleus stained with NucView, (c) and (g) N@C-dots-FA fluorescent images, (d) and (h) the overlay fluorescence images. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of C-dots synthesized with different natural carbon sources and synthetic methods.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical-assisted green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from crab shell as targeted nanoprobes for cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-pot sonochemical method was developed for the green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots with nitrogen dopants (N@C-dots) derived from crab shells. The synthesized N@C-dots exhibited excellent water solubility with a fluorescence quantum yield of 14.5%. The N@C-dots then conjugated with folic acid (FA) to construct the N@C-dots-FA nanoprobe with the ability to selectively target folate-receptor positive cancer cells. Moreover, in vitro cytotoxicity studies showed excellent biocompatibility. Cellular uptake of the N@C-dots-FA was further studied via in vitro confocal microscopy studies. Results showed that N@C-dots-FA nanoprobes were mostly localized in the cytoplasm of the folate receptor-overexpressing Human cervical cancer cell (HeLa) while cellular uptake by human embryo lung cell (HEL) cells (folate receptor-deficient control) was negligible. Based on their improved diagnostic imaging and enhanced targeting effect, the N@C-dots-FA probe has high potential in the theranostic applications. This study demonstrates an economically viable approach to synthesize N@C-dots-FA probe using seafood waste.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent developments in fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted enormous attention owing to their unique physicochemical properties including biocompatibility, low toxicity, water solubility, and photoluminescence [1–4]. Such excellent features combined in a single nano-dot, make them promising materials for versatile applications such as fluorescent markers, bio-imaging, drug delivery, sensing and medical diagnosis [2–5]. Over past decade, several methods like laser ablation of graphite [5], microwave synthesis [6,7] , thermal decomposition [8], electrochemical treatment [9,10], hydrothermal treatment [11] and template-based routes [12,13] have been explored to synthesize C-dots. These strategies are either relying on strong acids/bases and expensive instruments or requiring long reaction time and further surface-passivation steps.

Increasing environmental awareness has led to growing interest in recycling biomass wastes, once considered as an environmental burden for the society, as alternative sources for the green production of renewable, low cost, and sustainable nanomaterials. For instance, significant efforts have been devoted to producing fluorescent C-dots in an economical and environmentally friendly approach using inexpensive and renewable resources like bio-wastes [2,14–17] to replace graphite, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and organic chemicals precursors. Although these green C-dots are good examples of turning bio-waste into invaluable C-dots, they exhibited low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) that, in turn, motivated many groups to simultaneously search for new resources and modify the synthetic procedures to improve QY of green C-dots.

Presently, high consumption of seafood left over many wastes that are improperly discarded into the sea or dumped in landfills. While proper disposal of such solid waste is a challenging environmental issue, a great deal of research is focusing on efficient solutions not to resolve this issue but instead convert them into valuable materials [18–20]. For instance, crab shells constitute of nitrogen element as a form of chitin, a poly-b (1/4)-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine polysaccharides, and proteins can act as starting materials to prepare C-dots for uranium elimination [21] and lithium–sulfur batteries [22]. In our previous work, we developed a multifunctional hybrid composed of C-dots, derived from waste crab shell and doped with transition metal ions [6]. The theranostic C-dots were prepared using one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis at a high temperature wherein waste crab shell acted not only as a cheap source of carbon but also as a chelating agent to form complexes with metal ions.

In this study, we demonstrate a facile ‘green solution’ for the disposal of industrial waste crab shell and production of N@C-dots via a sonochemical method. The entire synthetic procedure is rapid, reproducible, and scalable leading to the synthesis of N@C-dots with excellent photoluminescence properties and low toxicity. Moreover, the results corroborated that subsequent conjugation of C-dots with folic acid (FA) facilitates their recognition and cellular uptake by cancer cells as investigated by fluorescence imaging.

Ethyl (dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 99%) and N‑hydroxy sulfo-succinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS, 97%) were obtained from Alfa-Aesar. FA (>98%) was acquired from TCI. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, 97.5%), was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. NucView Green nucleic acid stain (NucView) was purchased from Gene Copoeia. Crab shells were obtained from a local supermarket.

Crab shells were washed with tap water to remove flesh and other impurities and then dried at 70 °C for 12 h. Consequently, the dried crab shells were ground into a fine powder using an agate mortar and pestle. The fine powder (0.25 g) was dispersed in 20 mL of water, followed by ultrasonication irradiation using an ultrasonic probe (VCX 130 PB, 20 kHz, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newton, CT). The solution was then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.22-µm filter membrane to remove large particles. Finally, the products were purified by centrifugation (1 K MWCO, Macrosep Advance Centrifugal Device) at 2000 rpm for 60 min, to obtain N@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of N@C-dots with FA targeting ligand
ce:section.ce:para: A zero-length crosslinking approach using EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemistry was employed to activate N@C-dots functional groups and conjugate with FA. To activate the carboxylic group of N@C-dots, EDC (15 mg) and Sulfo-NHS (25 mg) in 1 mL of MES buffer were added to 2 mL of N@C-dots solution (15 mg/mL) and then stirred in the dark at room temperature for 40 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of MES solution containing FA (2.26 × 10−3 mmol) was added to the activated N@C-dots solution, and the mixture was stirred gently at room temperature in the dark. After 24 h, a Float-A-Lyzer G2 dialysis device (MWCO 500–1000 Da) was used to remove unreacted materials, and the final product (N@C-dots-FA) was obtained.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was performed on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (Philips, Amsterdam, Holland) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific) with Al Kα (1486 eV) as the X-ray excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a UniG2D Raman Spectroscope (UniNano Tech, Taiwan) with a 532 nm CW laser as the light source. UV−visible and fluorescence spectra were acquired on a JASCO V-630 and Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer, respectively. The fluorescent QY was calculated using quinine sulfate (QY = 95%) as a reference standard. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a PYRIS Diamond thermogravimetric instrument at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a flow of nitrogen.

Human cervical cancer cell (HeLa) and human embryo lung cell (HEL) were cultured in Minimum Essential Medium Eagle (MEM) accompanied with 1% L-glutamine, 1% antibiotic/antimycotic, 1% non-essential amino acids, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 10% fetal bovine serum. The cells were incubated in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37 °C for 24 h.

Cell viability of N@C-dots and N@C-dots-FA was estimated by using the 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-Diphenyl tetrazolium Bromide (MTT) colorimetric assay kit. The HeLa cells were harvested in 12-well plate with a density of ∼4 × 104 /well followed by pre-incubated at humidified incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then divided into two groups with three replicates in each group, and fresh medium contains various quantities of N@C-dots and N@C-dots-FA (0 to 1000 µg/mL) replaced the culture medium. After 24 h incubation, 1 mL of an MTT aqueous solution (500 µg/mL in PBS, 1 mL per well) was added to each well, followed by 4 h incubation at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator. The culture was then discarded, and 1 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide was used to lyse the HeLa cells. The absorbance at 570 nm was recorded using a microplate reader (BioTek Power wave XS, Winooski, VT, USA). Every experiment was performed three times under similar conditions. The following equation was used to calculate cell viability (%):
Cell viability (%) = (Optical density of treated sample / Optical density of control sample)×100%.

Cells were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of ∼5 × 104 cells/well for 24 h. Then, the medium was detached, and 0.5 mL of fresh culture medium containing N@C-dots-FA (500 µg/mL) was added to the wells for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) again and then fixed with 2 mL of 75% alcohol for 10 min. Nuclear staining was carried out by incubating the fixed cells with NucView (2 mL, 0.05 µg/mL in PBS) for 17 min. The cells were then washed three times with 10 mM PBS. Fluorescence images of the cells were visualized with a Leica TCS SP2 confocal laser scanning microscope.

Fig. 1 illustrates N@C-dots formation via a sonochemical approach using crab shell powder in an aqueous medium. In a green synthetic method, ultrasonic waves are used to generate alternating high-pressure as well as low-pressure waves in a liquid medium. This leads to the formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of microbubbles in the scale of microseconds, which is known as cavitation phenomena. The collapse of these bubbles generates high local temperatures and pressures that may reach up to 5000 K and 1000 atm, respectively [23]. When the crab shell powder is irradiated with ultrasound waves, the alternating expansive and compressive acoustic waves creates bubbles and induce their oscillation in liquids medium [24]. The oscillating bubbles accumulate ultrasonic energy, grow to tens of millimeter in size, and subsequently collapse. From this, the concentrated energy stored in the bubble releases at a short time, which ultimately lead to the formation of N@C-dots.

The ground powder of carb shells was made using a standard agate lab mortar and pestle which generally leads to low yield production of carbon microparticles with weak PL emission. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), PL intensity of the aqueous solution of purified ground powder is much lower than sonochemical treated samples. Higher ultrasonic power generates more cavitation bubbles and speeds up the diffusion by decreasing the mass transfer resistance. In fact, the chemical effect of the ultrasonic waves arises from acoustic cavitation in the formation, growth, and implosive collapse of the bubbles in a liquid medium. Accordingly, the PL intensity (Fig. 2(a)) increased with the increase of the ultrasonic power from 50 W to 120 W.

Therefore, ultrasound power and irradiation time influence size and dispersion of particles as well as their surface chemistry [25–27]. This indicates the formation of carbon dots with self-passivation and nitrogen doping that lead to the improvement of QY.

The Raman spectrum of N@C-dots (Fig. 2(b)) showed two characteristic bands, where the G band at 1574 cm–1 confirms the presence of the sp3 carbon, and the D band at 1350 cm–1 is attributed to partially disorder structures in the sp2 -hybridized carbon [28]. In general, the intensity ratio of ID/IG in the Raman spectrum is related to the disorder in the graphene materials. An ID/IG ratio of ∼0.92 could be attributed to the nitrogen doping into the C-dots framework. Also, the broad peak stems from the size distribution of C-dots and their variation of edge functionalized moieties.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the crab shell derived N@C-dots form spherical shape nanoparticles with an average size of 8 nm (Fig. 3(a) and (b)). The HR-TEM image of a single N@C-dots in (Fig. 3(c)) depicts a single contiguous crystalline phase. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d), the lattice fringes with a D-spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) facet of the graphene [29]. The surface chemistry and elemental composition of N@C-dots were investigated with XPS. The full-scan XPS survey spectrum of N@C-dots in Fig. 4 (a) showed three peaks at 293.4, 403.36, and 537.4 eV, attributed to carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the O1s peaks at 536, 537.2, 538.1 eV corresponding to O=COH [30], NO2 [31], and CO [32], respectively. The presence of nitrogen ions in N@C-dots was also detected by N1s signals (Fig. 3(c)) with binding energy values of 404.4 for oxidized pyridine nitrogen [33] and 405.4 eV due to the presence of NO2 group [34]. Peaks at 289.45, 290.2, and 290.78 eV can be assigned to carbon in the form of C=O, C–C/CN, and C=C, respectively [30]. The XPS results reveal that N@C-dots possess polar groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which render them water soluble. These functional groups are also advantageous in further functionalization of N@C-dots.

The UV–visible spectra of N@C-dots in Fig. 5 (a) shows two characteristic absorption peaks around 260 nm that are originated from (n→π*) transition of surface state region. This peak is observed for both ground crab shell and N@C-dots samples. As depicted in the inset of Fig. 5(b), the aqueous dispersion of the N@C-dots is transparent to daylight, whereas under UV lamp (λmax 360 nm) it shows intense blue fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra of the ground crab shell powder showed the maximum emission wavelength at 450 nm and featured an excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence emission. This excitation-independent emission behavior infers the similar surface state of the carbon dots extracted from the ground powder [35–38]. On the contrary, the N@C-dots emissions were excitation wavelength dependent, with the fluorescence band maximum red-shifted with the excitation at longer wavelengths.

The shift in the emission color as a function of the excitation wavelength arises from the structural inhomogeneity and availability of surface traps at different energies that has been comprehensively studied in the literature [2,3,16,39]. It is likely that the self-passivated oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N@C-dots are responsible for the efficient PL and enhanced QY of ∼14%. As shown in Table 1 , considering short reaction time and relatively high QY in comparison with previously reported carbon dots prepared from natural sources or food wastes, this method is a green and an economically viable approach for the synthesis of highly fluorescent N@C-dots.

Folate-receptors are important targets for the efficient delivery of drugs by means of FA ligands. The EDS/NHS coupling reaction was used to activate carboxylic group on N@C-dots surfaces and functionalize with FA through the formation of an amide bond. UV–Vis absorption and FT-IR spectroscopies were further employed to confirm the conjugation of FA with N@C-dots. From the Fig. 5(a), the appearance of a new peak at 210 [40] and higher absorption at 280 nm characteristic absorption peaks of FA indicated the functionalization of FA onto the surface of the N@C-dots [41]. The FT-IR spectrum of N@C-dots (Fig. 6 (a)), displayed a peak at 3398, 2930, 1640, 1563, and 1415 cm−1 specifying stretching vibrations of − OH and N − H stretching, C–H stretching, C=O stretching, N–H bending, and C=C stretching, respectively [41]. The FT-IR spectra of free FA exhibited the characteristic absorption peak at 1692 cm−1 (C=O stretching from –COOH) along with 1516 and 1483 cm−1 corresponding to the phenyl and pterin rings [42,43]. After conjugation of FA, the stretching vibrations of –OH and NH broad bands at 3398 cm−1 in N@C-dots shifted to 3419 cm−1 in N@C-dots-FA. Moreover, the peaks indicating C=O stretching and N−H bending vibrations shifted to 1658 and 1569 cm−1, respectively.

Apart from spectroscopic analyses, the TGA experiment was employed to confirm conjugation of FA and N@C-dots. TGA curves of N@C-dots and N@C-dots- FA are shown in Fig. 6(b). Unlike the N@C-dots, the N@C-dots -FA conjugates shows losses at various temperatures. A weight loss of approximately 19.5% was observed between 250 and 350 °C, possibly due to the loss detachment of FA [53]. Altogether, the UV−vis absorption, FT-IR, and TGA results confirm successful conjugation of FA onto the N@C-dots surface.

The cytotoxicity of the N@C-dots and N@C-dots-FA was evaluated with HeLa cell lines using in vitro MTT assay. The cells were exposed to various concentrations (20–1000 µg/mL) of N@C-dots and N@C-dots-FA for 24 h (Fig. 7 (a) and (b)). There were no noticeable changes in cell viability even at a high concentration of 1000 µg/mL. Precisely, the viabilities of HeLa cells were higher than 90%. These results indicate low toxicity of N@C-dots-FA to the HeLa cells and show their excellent biocompatibility to serve as imaging probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N@C-dots-FA as a targeted probe for bio-imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To further examine N@C-dots-FA function as a fluorescence targeted probe, their targeting to Hela cells as folate receptor-positive cells were studied in vitro. The expression level of folate receptor in cancer cells is higher than normal cells [54,55] which makes folate receptor overexpression an important tool in anticancer targeting. In this regard, HEL cells were used as a control group to assess the N@C-dots-FA selective targeting to HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. 8 , both cell lines were treated with N@C-dots-FA and visualized under confocal microscopy.

In order to study the cellular uptake, NucView was utilized to stain nuclei as the green fluorescence signal in Fig 8(b) visualizes them. As depicted in Fig. 5(b), N@C-dots-FA emit an intense blue color under UV irradiation. Likewise, the confocal image of the probe showed the blue fluorescence signal, an indication of facile N@C-dots-FA uptake by folate receptor-overexpressing HeLa cells. To further verify the localization of the probe, the overlay fluorescence was taken. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the blue fluorescence signals were mostly localized in the cytoplasm that was stained with NucView green signal.

In contrast, the blue signal of N@C-dots-FA in Fig. 8(g) is very weak for the HEL healthy cells. According to the overly image, Fig. 8(h), N@C-dots-FA accumulation inside HEL cells was also negligible. Consequently, N@C-dots-FA enhanced selective targeting towards cancer cells that may reduce the applicable dosage of the imaging agent and thereby minimize the side effects. These results strongly suggest that N@C-dots-FA feature a cost-effective, biocompatible, and highly fluorescent probe for targeted bio-imaging applications.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated N@C-dots using waste crab shell through a rapid, green, and convenient one-pot sonochemical approach. The synthesized N@C-dots exhibited excellent biocompatibility and fluorescent intensity. Due to their hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups, the N@C-dots possess excellent water solubility, which further facilitated conjugating with FA targeting group in the construction of N@C-dots-FA nanoprobes. The confocal microscopy studies demonstrated that N@C-dots-FA could serve as an efficient fluorescent imaging agent to specifically target cancer cells. We expect the green fabrication of biocompatible N@C-dots by recycling wastes will promote C-dots diagnostic and theranostic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of the Republic of China (MOST 107-2113-M-011-002). This work was financially supported by the Taiwan Building Technology Center from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education in Taiwan.
